Microfinance programs, women's cooperatives, and land tenure reform have improved women's participation and productivity in agriculture. Students must analyze how gender inequality affects agricultural production and food security. Connect to demographic change, economic development, population policies, and cultural patterns. AP exam note: analyzing the role of women in agriculture and development is increasingly important. Vocabulary: gender gap, land tenure, microfinance, cooperative, extension services, subsistence farming, food security, empowerment, FAO, labor force participation.

Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.skill: PSO-6.A (Unit 6, Topic 6.1) Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and Processes - 6.1 The Origin and Influences of Urbanization
Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.prompt: This topic covers the origin and influences of urbanization for AP Human Geography students (CED: PSO-6.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.1). Students should understand that urbanization is the process by which an increasing proportion of a population lives in cities and suburbs. The first cities emerged around 3500 BCE in hearth regions including Mesopotamia, the Nile Valley, the Indus Valley, the Yellow River Valley, and Mesoamerica, enabled by agricultural surplus from the First Agricultural Revolution. The Industrial Revolution (18th-19th centuries) triggered massive urbanization in Europe and North America as factories concentrated in cities, drawing rural workers seeking employment. Today, urbanization is most rapid in developing countries in Africa and Asia, driven by rural-to-urban migration, natural population increase, and economic restructuring. The world became majority urban around 2008. Factors influencing urbanization include economic opportunity (pull factor), agricultural mechanization reducing rural labor needs (push factor), access to education and healthcare, and social and cultural attractions of cities. Students must trace the historical development of urbanization and explain the factors driving contemporary urban growth. Connect to the Industrial Revolution, the DTM, agriculture, and globalization. AP exam note: understanding urbanization causes across historical periods is essential. Vocabulary: urbanization, urban hearth, agricultural surplus, rural-to-urban migration, industrialization, megacity, primate city, urban sprawl, suburb, metropolitan area.

Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.skill: PSO-6.B (Unit 6, Topic 6.2) Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and Processes - 6.2 Cities Across the World
Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.prompt: This topic covers cities across the world for AP Human Geography students (CED: PSO-6.B, Unit 6, Topic 6.2). Students should understand that cities in different world regions exhibit distinct characteristics shaped by their historical development, cultural traditions, colonial legacies, and economic context. North American cities typically feature a central business district (CBD), suburban sprawl, automobile-dependent transportation, and grid street patterns. European cities feature historic cores, higher density, extensive public transit, and preserved architectural heritage. Latin American cities follow the Griffin-Ford model with a CBD, commercial spine extending outward, zones of maturity, in situ accretion, and peripheral squatter settlements. Southeast Asian cities follow the McGee model (desakota) with mixed urban and rural land uses extending along transportation corridors. African cities reflect colonial planning with distinct European and indigenous sectors and rapidly growing informal settlements. Islamic cities traditionally center on the mosque and bazaar with winding street patterns. Students must compare urban forms across regions and explain how historical and cultural factors produce distinct urban patterns. Connect to colonialism, globalization, cultural landscapes, and economic development. AP exam note: applying city models to specific world regions is commonly tested. Vocabulary: CBD, Griffin-Ford model, McGee model, desakota, medina, bazaar, colonial city, informal settlement, squatter settlement, suburb, urban morphology.

Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.skill: IMP-6.A (Unit 6, Topic 6.3) Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and Processes - 6.3 Cities and Globalization
Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.prompt: This topic covers cities and globalization for AP Human Geography students (CED: IMP-6.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.3). Students should understand that globalization has transformed cities into nodes in a global network of economic, cultural, and political interaction. World cities (global cities) such as New York, London, Tokyo, and Paris serve as command centers for the global economy, hosting headquarters of transnational corporations, major financial markets, international organizations, and global media. These cities are classified into tiers based on their degree of global connectivity and influence. Globalization has produced new urban landscapes including international financial districts, technology hubs, and global consumption spaces. Economic restructuring has shifted employment from manufacturing to service-sector and knowledge-based industries in developed-world cities, creating both high-paying professional jobs and low-wage service positions. Cities in developing countries are increasingly integrated into global production networks as sites for manufacturing and outsourced services. The competition among cities to attract global investment drives urban development policies, sometimes at the expense of equity and sustainability. Students must analyze how globalization has reshaped urban form, function, and hierarchy. Connect to economic development, trade, migration, and cultural diffusion. AP exam note: understanding global city functions and hierarchies is commonly tested. Vocabulary: world city, global city, globalization, transnational corporation, deindustrialization, outsourcing, service sector, global hierarchy, economic restructuring, foreign direct investment.

Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.skill: PSO-6.C (Unit 6, Topic 6.4) Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and Processes - 6.4 The Size and Distribution of Cities
Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.prompt: This topic covers the size and distribution of cities for AP Human Geography students (CED: PSO-6.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.4). Students should understand the models and concepts geographers use to analyze urban hierarchies and distribution patterns. The rank-size rule states that the nth largest city in a country will be 1/n the size of the largest city, producing a smooth distribution of city sizes (e.g., the United States). A primate city is at least twice the size of the next largest city and dominates the country's economic, political, and cultural life (e.g., Paris in France, Bangkok in Thailand, Mexico City in Mexico). The gravity model predicts that interaction between two cities is proportional to the product of their populations and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. Christaller's central place theory explains the size, number, and spacing of settlements based on the range (maximum distance consumers will travel) and threshold (minimum market needed) for goods and services, producing a hexagonal pattern. Students must apply these models to analyze real-world urban systems and evaluate their strengths and limitations. Connect to urbanization, economic geography, and transportation networks. AP exam note: applying rank-size rule, primate city, and central place theory is essential. Vocabulary: rank-size rule, primate city, gravity model, central place theory, Christaller, range, threshold, hexagonal pattern, urban hierarchy, hinterland.

Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.skill: PSO-6.D (Unit 6, Topic 6.5) Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and Processes - 6.5 The Internal Structure of Cities
Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.prompt: This topic covers the internal structure of cities for AP Human Geography students (CED: PSO-6.D, Unit 6, Topic 6.5). Students should understand the classic models that explain the spatial organization of land use within cities. The concentric zone model (Burgess, 1925) proposes that cities grow outward in rings from the CBD, with zones including the CBD, zone of transition, working-class housing, middle-class housing, and commuter zone. The sector model (Hoyt, 1939) suggests that land use develops in wedge-shaped sectors extending outward from the CBD along transportation routes, with high-income housing, industrial areas, and low-income housing forming distinct sectors. The multiple nuclei model (Harris and Ullman, 1945) argues that cities develop around several distinct centers (nuclei) of activity, each attracting compatible land uses. The galactic city model (edge city model) reflects contemporary suburban development with peripheral business and commercial centers connected by highways. Students must apply these models to analyze the internal structure of specific cities and evaluate each model's explanatory power. Connect to urbanization, transportation, socioeconomic patterns, and land use. AP exam note: comparing and applying all urban models is a core skill. Vocabulary: concentric zone model, sector model, multiple nuclei model, galactic city, Burgess, Hoyt, Harris and Ullman, CBD, edge city, zone of transition.

Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.skill: PSO-6.E (Unit 6, Topic 6.6) Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and Processes - 6.6 Density and Land Use
Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.prompt: This topic covers density and land use for AP Human Geography students (CED: PSO-6.E, Unit 6, Topic 6.6). Students should understand that urban density and land use patterns reflect economic, cultural, and policy decisions that shape the built environment. Bid rent theory explains that land values are highest at the CBD and decline with distance, determining which activities can afford locations at different distances from the center. Commercial uses outbid residential and industrial uses near the CBD due to the benefits of accessibility and centrality. Zoning laws regulate land use by designating areas for residential, commercial, industrial, or mixed use. High-density housing (apartments, condominiums) typically locates near the urban core, while low-density housing (single-family homes) dominates suburbs. Urban sprawl describes the expansion of low-density development into surrounding rural areas, driven by automobile dependence, highway construction, and consumer preferences. New urbanism and smart growth movements promote compact, walkable, mixed-use communities as alternatives to sprawl. Students must analyze how density gradients and land use regulations shape urban form and evaluate the consequences of different development patterns. Connect to transportation, infrastructure, sustainability, and urban models. AP exam note: applying bid rent theory to urban land use is essential. Vocabulary: bid rent theory, zoning, urban density, urban sprawl, new urbanism, smart growth, mixed-use development, gentrification, greenfield, brownfield, suburb.

Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.skill: IMP-6.B (Unit 6, Topic 6.7) Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and Processes - 6.7 Infrastructure
Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.prompt: This topic covers infrastructure for AP Human Geography students (CED: IMP-6.B, Unit 6, Topic 6.7). Students should understand that urban infrastructure encompasses the physical systems that support city function, including transportation networks, water supply and sewage systems, electrical grids, communication networks, and public facilities. Transportation infrastructure (roads, highways, rail, public transit, airports) shapes urban form and accessibility, influencing where people live and work. Cities in developed countries generally have extensive, well-maintained infrastructure, while cities in developing countries often face infrastructure deficits including inadequate water treatment, unreliable electricity, unpaved roads, and insufficient public transit. Infrastructure investment decisions affect equity: historically, highway construction in the US displaced minority communities and connected suburbs while fragmenting inner-city neighborhoods. Public transit systems promote accessibility and reduce automobile dependence and environmental impacts. Digital infrastructure (broadband, cell networks) increasingly influences economic competitiveness and social inclusion. Students must analyze how infrastructure investment shapes urban development, accessibility, and quality of life. Connect to urbanization, sustainability, economic development, and social equity. AP exam note: analyzing infrastructure's role in urban development and its uneven distribution is important. Vocabulary: infrastructure, public transit, highway, water supply, sanitation, broadband, accessibility, equity, transportation network, utility.

Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.skill: IMP-6.C (Unit 6, Topic 6.8) Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and Processes - 6.8 Urban Sustainability
Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.prompt: This topic covers urban sustainability for AP Human Geography students (CED: IMP-6.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.8). Students should understand that urban sustainability involves designing and managing cities to meet current needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. Sustainable urban strategies include green building (LEED certification), renewable energy integration, public transit investment, bike-sharing and pedestrian infrastructure, urban green spaces and parks, rainwater harvesting, and waste reduction and recycling programs. Brownfield remediation converts contaminated industrial sites into productive community spaces. Transit-oriented development (TOD) concentrates residential and commercial development around transit stations to reduce automobile dependence. Smart growth policies limit urban sprawl by promoting infill development and compact urban design. Green belts and urban growth boundaries prevent outward expansion and preserve agricultural and natural lands. Ecological footprint analysis measures the environmental impact of urban consumption patterns. Students must evaluate urban sustainability initiatives and analyze the trade-offs between economic development, environmental protection, and social equity. Connect to infrastructure, urbanization, globalization, and environmental challenges. AP exam note: analyzing specific sustainability strategies and their effectiveness is commonly tested. Vocabulary: sustainability, green building, brownfield, transit-oriented development, smart growth, green belt, urban growth boundary, ecological footprint, LEED, infill development.

Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.skill: PSO-6.F (Unit 6, Topic 6.9) Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and Processes - 6.9 Urban Data
Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.prompt: This topic covers urban data for AP Human Geography students (CED: PSO-6.F, Unit 6, Topic 6.9). Students should understand the quantitative and qualitative data sources geographers use to study urban areas. Census data provides population counts, demographic characteristics, housing data, and economic information at various geographic scales (block, tract, county, metropolitan area). Satellite imagery and remote sensing allow analysis of urban growth patterns, land cover change, and environmental conditions. GIS layers urban data to reveal spatial patterns in housing values, crime rates, demographic composition, and access to services. Census tracts and block groups enable fine-grained spatial analysis of urban neighborhoods. Qualitative methods including fieldwork, interviews, and photographic documentation capture the human experience of urban spaces. Urban data is used to calculate metrics such as population density, housing vacancy rates, poverty rates, commuting patterns, and access to amenities. The American Community Survey supplements the decennial census with annual demographic estimates. Students must interpret urban data sources and explain how they inform urban planning and policy decisions. Connect to GIS, maps, scales of analysis, and urban models. AP exam note: interpreting urban data in maps, tables, and graphs is a key skill. Vocabulary: census tract, GIS, remote sensing, population density, metropolitan statistical area, commuting pattern, demographic data, land cover, vacancy rate, urban indicator.

Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.skill: IMP-6.D (Unit 6, Topic 6.10) Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and Processes - 6.10 Challenges of Urban Changes
Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.prompt: This topic covers challenges of urban changes for AP Human Geography students (CED: IMP-6.D, Unit 6, Topic 6.10). Students should understand that urban areas face interconnected challenges resulting from growth, decline, and transformation. Suburbanization and urban sprawl contribute to automobile dependence, environmental degradation, loss of farmland, and social segregation. Deindustrialization in cities of the developed world has caused unemployment, population loss, abandoned properties, and declining tax bases (e.g., Detroit, Cleveland, Pittsburgh). Gentrification occurs when higher-income residents move into lower-income neighborhoods, renovating housing and increasing property values, which can displace long-term residents and change neighborhood character. Residential segregation by race, ethnicity, and income persists due to historical policies (redlining, restrictive covenants), market forces, and individual preferences. Squatter settlements and informal housing in developing-world cities lack legal land tenure, adequate infrastructure, and public services but represent essential housing for millions. Students must analyze the causes, processes, and consequences of urban changes in both developed and developing contexts. Connect to urbanization models, infrastructure, sustainability, and economic development. AP exam note: analyzing gentrification and segregation processes and impacts is frequently tested. Vocabulary: suburbanization, deindustrialization, gentrification, redlining, segregation, squatter settlement, blight, displacement, urban renewal, white flight.

Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.skill: IMP-6.E (Unit 6, Topic 6.11) Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and Processes - 6.11 Challenges of Urban Sustainability
Cities and Urban Land-Use Patterns and P.prompt: This topic covers challenges of urban sustainability for AP Human Geography students (CED: IMP-6.E, Unit 6, Topic 6.11). Students should understand that achieving urban sustainability requires addressing economic, social, and environmental challenges simultaneously. Environmental challenges include air and water pollution, urban heat island effects (cities being significantly warmer than surrounding rural areas), waste management for millions of residents, loss of green space, and vulnerability to climate-change impacts such as flooding and extreme heat. Social challenges include housing affordability, homelessness, unequal access to public services and green spaces, food deserts in low-income neighborhoods, and environmental justice concerns where polluting facilities disproportionately impact minority communities. Economic challenges include maintaining infrastructure, generating sufficient tax revenue, attracting investment while ensuring equitable development, and managing the costs of urban growth or decline. Developing-world cities face the additional challenge of rapid urbanization outpacing infrastructure development, creating vast informal settlements lacking basic services. Students must evaluate the interconnected nature of sustainability challenges and analyze strategies that address multiple dimensions. Connect to urban data, infrastructure, urban models, and environmental sustainability. AP exam note: analyzing sustainability challenges with specific case studies is a key free-response topic. Vocabulary: urban heat island, environmental justice, food desert, housing affordability, waste management, climate resilience, informal settlement, green space, equity, pollution.

Industrial and Economic Development Patt.skill: SPS-7.A (Unit 7, Topic 7.1) Industrial and Economic Development Patterns and Processes - 7.1 The Industrial Revolution
Industrial and Economic Development Patt.prompt: This topic covers the Industrial Revolution for AP Human Geography students (CED: SPS-7.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.1). Students should understand that the Industrial Revolution began in England in the late 18th century and fundamentally transformed economies from agrarian to industrial, reshaping global economic geography. Key factors enabling industrialization in Britain included abundant coal and iron resources, colonial markets and raw materials, a skilled labor force, capital accumulation from trade, navigable rivers and ports, and political stability. The revolution diffused from Britain to Western Europe, North America, and eventually to other world regions through hierarchical and relocation diffusion. Weber's least cost theory explains industrial location based on minimizing transportation costs, labor costs, and agglomeration effects. Agglomeration economies occur when industries cluster together, sharing infrastructure, labor pools, and suppliers. Deglomeration occurs when disadvantages of clustering (congestion, high costs, pollution) push industries to relocate. Fordism describes the assembly-line mass production model that dominated 20th-century manufacturing. Students must analyze the spatial patterns and factors of industrialization. Connect to urbanization, economic development, agriculture, and globalization. AP exam note: applying Weber's model and understanding industrial location factors is essential. Vocabulary: Industrial Revolution, Weber, least cost theory, agglomeration, deglomeration, Fordism, bulk-reducing, bulk-gaining, break-of-bulk, site factors, situation factors.

Industrial and Economic Development Patt.skill: PSO-7.A (Unit 7, Topic 7.2) Industrial and Economic Development Patterns and Processes - 7.2 Economic Sectors and Patterns
Industrial and Economic Development Patt.prompt: This topic covers economic sectors and patterns for AP Human Geography students (CED: PSO-7.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.2). Students should understand that economies are organized into sectors that reflect different types of economic activity and levels of development. The primary sector extracts raw materials from the earth (agriculture, mining, fishing, forestry). The secondary sector transforms raw materials into finished goods (manufacturing, construction, processing). The tertiary sector provides services (retail, healthcare, education, transportation, hospitality). The quaternary sector involves knowledge-based activities (research, information technology, consulting, financial services). The quinary sector involves high-level decision-making (government leadership, top corporate executives, scientific research). As countries develop economically, their workforce shifts from primary to secondary to tertiary and quaternary sectors, a process known as sectoral transformation. Developed countries have large tertiary and quaternary sectors, while developing countries have large primary sectors. Post-industrial economies rely heavily on services and knowledge production. Students must identify economic sectors, analyze workforce distribution across sectors, and relate sectoral patterns to development levels. Connect to measures of development, industrialization, and globalization. AP exam note: classifying activities by sector and analyzing sectoral shifts is commonly tested. Vocabulary: primary sector, secondary sector, tertiary sector, quaternary sector, quinary sector, sectoral transformation, post-industrial, deindustrialization, economic structure, labor force.

Industrial and Economic Development Patt.skill: PSO-7.B (Unit 7, Topic 7.3) Industrial and Economic Development Patterns and Processes - 7.3 Measures of Development
Industrial and Economic Development Patt.prompt: This topic covers measures of development for AP Human Geography students (CED: PSO-7.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.3). Students should understand the various indicators used to measure and compare economic development across countries. Gross Domestic Product (GDP) measures the total value of goods and services produced within a country; GDP per capita divides this by population for a per-person comparison. Gross National Income (GNI) includes income earned by citizens abroad. The Human Development Index (HDI) combines life expectancy, education (mean and expected years of schooling), and GNI per capita to provide a composite measure of human well-being. The Gender Inequality Index (GII) measures gender-based disadvantages in reproductive health, empowerment, and labor market participation. Other indicators include literacy rate, infant mortality rate, access to healthcare, poverty rate, and income distribution (Gini coefficient). No single measure captures all dimensions of development, and different indicators may give different rankings. The distinction between developed (more developed countries, MDCs) and developing (less developed countries, LDCs) oversimplifies a complex spectrum. Students must interpret development data, compare countries using multiple indicators, and evaluate the strengths and limitations of each measure. Connect to economic sectors, theories of development, and globalization. AP exam note: interpreting and comparing development indicators is a core skill. Vocabulary: GDP, GNI, HDI, GII, Gini coefficient, literacy rate, IMR, life expectancy, MDC, LDC, purchasing power parity.

Industrial and Economic Development Patt.skill: IMP-7.A (Unit 7, Topic 7.4) Industrial and Economic Development Patterns and Processes - 7.4 Women and Economic Development
Industrial and Economic Development Patt.prompt: This topic covers women and economic development for AP Human Geography students (CED: IMP-7.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.4). Students should understand that the status and economic participation of women is both an indicator and driver of development. The Gender Inequality Index (GII) measures disparities in reproductive health (maternal mortality, adolescent birth rate), empowerment (parliamentary representation, educational attainment), and labor force participation. Countries with greater gender equality tend to have higher levels of economic development, lower fertility rates, better health outcomes, and higher educational attainment. Microfinance institutions (such as Grameen Bank founded by Muhammad Yunus) provide small loans to women entrepreneurs in developing countries, enabling them to start businesses and achieve economic independence. Women's employment in export-processing zones and garment factories has increased economic participation but often involves low wages and poor working conditions. Education of girls and women is one of the most effective development investments, producing multiplier effects across health, family size, and economic productivity. Students must analyze how gender equality relates to development indicators and evaluate programs designed to empower women economically. Connect to population dynamics, agricultural development, and development theories. AP exam note: analyzing gender and development connections using specific data is commonly tested. Vocabulary: Gender Inequality Index, microfinance, Grameen Bank, empowerment, labor force participation, maternal mortality, gender gap, reproductive rights, education access, multiplier effect.

Industrial and Economic Development Patt.skill: PSO-7.C (Unit 7, Topic 7.5) Industrial and Economic Development Patterns and Processes - 7.5 Theories of Development
Industrial and Economic Development Patt.prompt: This topic covers theories of development for AP Human Geography students (CED: PSO-7.C, Unit 7, Topic 7.5). Students should understand the major theoretical frameworks that attempt to explain why some countries are wealthy and others are poor. Rostow's modernization theory (stages of economic growth) proposes a linear five-stage path from traditional society through preconditions for takeoff, takeoff, drive to maturity, and age of high mass consumption. This model assumes all countries can follow the Western development path. Wallerstein's world-systems theory divides the world into core (wealthy, industrialized nations), semi-periphery (middle-income, industrializing), and periphery (low-income, dependent on raw material exports) and argues that the core exploits the periphery in a structurally unequal global system. Dependency theory argues that developing countries remain poor because they are economically dependent on and exploited by wealthy nations through unfair trade, debt, and neocolonialism. Commodity dependence theory explains how reliance on a few export commodities creates vulnerability. Students must compare, contrast, and evaluate these theories using real-world evidence. Connect to trade, globalization, colonial legacies, and economic sectors. AP exam note: comparing Rostow and Wallerstein is a classic exam topic. Vocabulary: Rostow, modernization theory, Wallerstein, world-systems theory, core, periphery, semi-periphery, dependency theory, takeoff, commodity dependence, neocolonialism.

Industrial and Economic Development Patt.skill: IMP-7.B (Unit 7, Topic 7.6) Industrial and Economic Development Patterns and Processes - 7.6 Trade and the World Economy
Industrial and Economic Development Patt.prompt: This topic covers trade and the world economy for AP Human Geography students (CED: IMP-7.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.6). Students should understand that international trade is a fundamental driver of globalization and development, shaping economic relationships between countries. Free trade agreements (NAFTA/USMCA, EU single market) reduce tariffs and trade barriers between member states. The World Trade Organization (WTO) promotes free trade by negotiating trade rules and settling disputes. Comparative advantage theory (Ricardo) argues that countries benefit from specializing in products they can produce most efficiently relative to others. However, trade relationships often reflect and reinforce global inequalities: developing countries frequently export low-value raw materials and import high-value manufactured goods, creating unfavorable terms of trade. Trade blocs and economic unions (EU, ASEAN, African Union, Mercosur) create preferential trade arrangements. Foreign direct investment (FDI) from transnational corporations connects developing economies to global markets but can create dependency. Export-processing zones (EPZs) and special economic zones (SEZs) attract foreign investment through tax incentives and relaxed regulations. Students must analyze how trade patterns reflect and affect global development disparities. Connect to theories of development, globalization, economic sectors, and agriculture. AP exam note: analyzing trade patterns and their development implications is essential. Vocabulary: free trade, tariff, WTO, comparative advantage, terms of trade, trade bloc, FDI, export-processing zone, special economic zone, transnational corporation, outsourcing.

Industrial and Economic Development Patt.skill: IMP-7.C (Unit 7, Topic 7.7) Industrial and Economic Development Patterns and Processes - 7.7 Changes as a Result of the World Economy
Industrial and Economic Development Patt.prompt: This topic covers changes as a result of the world economy for AP Human Geography students (CED: IMP-7.C, Unit 7, Topic 7.7). Students should understand that integration into the global economy has produced significant spatial, economic, and social changes across the world. The new international division of labor (NIDL) describes the shift of manufacturing from developed to developing countries seeking lower wages, while developed countries specialize in services, research, and high-technology industries. Outsourcing and offshoring have relocated jobs across borders, benefiting some workers in developing countries while displacing workers in developed nations. Newly industrialized countries (NICs) such as South Korea, Taiwan, Singapore, and China have achieved rapid economic growth through export-oriented industrialization. Deindustrialization in developed countries has transformed urban landscapes, creating rust belts and economic hardship in former manufacturing centers. The informal economy (unreported, unregulated economic activity) is significant in developing countries. Structural adjustment programs (SAPs) imposed by the IMF and World Bank on developing countries have required market liberalization, privatization, and austerity measures, with controversial results. Students must analyze how global economic integration produces winners and losers at multiple scales. Connect to industrialization, urbanization, migration, and development theories. AP exam note: analyzing global economic changes with specific examples is a key skill. Vocabulary: NIDL, outsourcing, offshoring, NIC, deindustrialization, rust belt, informal economy, structural adjustment, privatization, austerity, export-oriented industrialization.

Industrial and Economic Development Patt.skill: IMP-7.D (Unit 7, Topic 7.8) Industrial and Economic Development Patterns and Processes - 7.8 Sustainability and the Environment
Industrial and Economic Development Patt.prompt: This topic covers sustainability and the environment for AP Human Geography students (CED: IMP-7.D, Unit 7, Topic 7.8). Students should understand that economic development and environmental sustainability are deeply interconnected and often in tension. The concept of sustainable development (Brundtland Commission, 1987) seeks to meet present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet their own. Climate change, driven by greenhouse gas emissions from industrialization, transportation, and deforestation, represents the greatest environmental challenge affecting all scales of human geography. The United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) provide a framework for addressing poverty, inequality, and environmental degradation simultaneously. Ecotourism promotes economic development while preserving natural environments and benefiting local communities. Carbon trading and cap-and-trade systems create market-based incentives for reducing emissions. The ecological footprint measures human demand on natural resources. Environmental challenges disproportionately affect developing countries and marginalized communities, raising issues of environmental justice. Renewable energy transition, conservation, and circular economy principles offer pathways toward sustainability. Students must analyze the relationship between development and environmental impact and evaluate sustainability strategies. Connect to agriculture, urbanization, industrialization, and global trade. AP exam note: analyzing sustainability challenges and solutions across scales is increasingly important. Vocabulary: sustainable development, climate change, greenhouse gas, SDGs, ecotourism, ecological footprint, environmental justice, renewable energy, carbon trading, Brundtland Commission, circular economy.
